Journal.Title: The British journal of psychiatry : the journal of mental science

Abstract.AbstractText: Shame is a pervasive, multidimensional emotion influencing brain, body and social life. While shame can foster accountability, its toxic forms drive stigma, withdrawal and mental illness. We call for systemic, culturally sensitive interventions to transform phatological shame into healing, fostering empathy, accountability and psychological safety in care, education and policy.

Reference.Citation: Sznycer D. Forms and functions of the self-conscious emotions. Trends Cogn Sci 2019; 23: 143–57.
Baez S, García AM, Santamaría-García H. Moral cognition and moral emotions. In Neuroscience and Social Science (eds Ibáñez A, Sedeño L, García AM): 169–97. SpringerLink, 2017.
Bastin C, Harrison BJ, Davey CG, Moll J, Whittle S. Feelings of shame, embarrassment and guilt and their neural correlates: a systematic review. Neurosci Biobehav Rev 2016; 71: 455–71.
Landers M, Sznycer D. The evolution of shame and its display. Evol Hum Sci 2022; 4: e45.
Schaumberg RL, Skowronek SE. Shame broadcasts social norms: the positive social effects of shame on norm acquisition and normative behavior. Psychol Sci 2022; 33: 1257–77.

Matos M, Galhardo A, Moura-Ramos M, Steindl SR, Bortolon C, Hiramatsu Y, et al. Measuring shame across five countries: dimensionality and measurement invariance of the external and internal shame scale. Curr Psychol 2023; 42: 7161–70.
Michail M, Birchwood M. Social anxiety disorder and shame cognitions in psychosis. Psychol Med 2013; 43: 133–42.
Matos M, Coimbra M, Ferreira C. When body dysmorphia symptomatology meets disordered eating: the role of shame and self-criticism. Appetite 2023; 186: 106552.
Dolezal L, Bynum W. Shame competence: addressing the effects of shame in health care. Lancet 2024; 404: 1514–5.
